generations.code:
Actually if N(c) \ {neighbor_d} is empty, robot stays - bad (unless c=lab).

So c is good if ∃ d such that:
- N(c) \ {neighbor_d} is non-empty (robot moves), AND
- All cells in N(c) \ {neighbor_d} are good.

Wait, if N(c) \ {neighbor_d} is empty, robot stays. That's only ok if c is lab.

BFS/multi-source: Start with lab as good. Process cells in BFS order.

For each cell, count how many neighbors are "not yet good". When a neighbor becomes good, update counts. A cell becomes good when there exists a direction d such that all other free neighbors are good AND at least one free neighbor exists in other directions.

Let me implement: for each cell c and each direction d, track count of non-good neighbors excluding direction d. When this count reaches 0 AND there's at least one free neighbor in other directions, cell c becomes good.

For each cell c, for each direction d: `bad_count[c][d]` = number of free neighbors of c in directions != d that are not yet good. Cell c becomes good when any `bad_count[c][d] == 0` AND `free_count[c][d] > 0` (where free_count is number of free neighbors excluding direction d).

When a cell `v` becomes good, for each neighbor `c` of `v` (in direction `dir_from_c_to_v`), for each direction `d != opposite(dir_from_c_to_v)` (i.e., d such that v is in direction != d from c, meaning dir_from_c_to_v != d): decrement `bad_count[c][d]`.

Actually: v is neighbor of c in direction `dir`. So v contributes to bad_count[c][d] for all d != dir. When v becomes good, decrement bad_count[c][d] for all d != dir.

Let me code this up.

Actually if N(c) \ {neighbor_d} is empty, robot stays - bad (unless c=lab).

So c is good if ∃ d such that:
- N(c) \ {neighbor_d} is non-empty (robot moves), AND
- All cells in N(c) \ {neighbor_d} are good.

Wait, if N(c) \ {neighbor_d} is empty, robot stays. That's only ok if c is lab.

BFS/multi-source: Start with lab as good. Process cells in BFS order.

For each cell, count how many neighbors are "not yet good". When a neighbor becomes good, update counts. A cell becomes good when there exists a direction d such that all other free neighbors are good AND at least one free neighbor exists in other directions.

Let me implement: for each cell c and each direction d, track count of non-good neighbors excluding direction d. When this count reaches 0 AND there's at least one free neighbor in other directions, cell c becomes good.

For each cell c, for each direction d: `bad_count[c][d]` = number of free neighbors of c in directions != d that are not yet good. Cell c becomes good when any `bad_count[c][d] == 0` AND `free_count[c][d] > 0` (where free_count is number of free neighbors excluding direction d).

When a cell `v` becomes good, for each neighbor `c` of `v` (in direction `dir_from_c_to_v`), for each direction `d != opposite(dir_from_c_to_v)` (i.e., d such that v is in direction != d from c, meaning dir_from_c_to_v != d): decrement `bad_count[c][d]`.

Actually: v is neighbor of c in direction `dir`. So v contributes to bad_count[c][d] for all d != dir. When v becomes good, decrement bad_count[c][d] for all d != dir.
